SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are solid: they evaluate the Visual CNN embeddings against a Lookup baseline across large multilingual Wikipedia-derived datasets (Chinese 593k, Japanese 810k, Korean 46.6k), test three fusion methods, and report classification accuracy improvements and qualitative analyses. They also report limitations (no clear gain in low-resource settings, poor script-transfer performance) and provide code and hyperparameters, supporting reproducibility. However, there is limited evidence of deeper ablations or sensitivity analyses on the CNN/visual-rendering choices (e.g., font, architecture, training size sweeps) beyond the fusion variants, so some important experimental avenues remain unexamined. | B(4): The paper presents solid and reasonably comprehensive experiments: multilingual CJK datasets (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) with 12-category Wikipedia classification, direct comparisons to a Lookup baseline, three fusion strategies, and qualitative analyses showing visual-focus and rare-character benefits. It also reports hyperparameters and releases code, and discusses limitations (CNN struggles with low-resource settings and script-transfer drops). However, it lacks deeper ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., on CNN architecture, image rendering choices, and more systematic low-resource/transfer experiments) that would strengthen the claims about when and why the visual model helps.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are solid: they evaluate the Visual CNN embeddings against a Lookup baseline across large multilingual Wikipedia-derived datasets (Chinese 593k, Japanese 810k, Korean 46.6k), test three fusion methods, and report classification accuracy improvements and qualitative analyses. They also report limitations (no clear gain in low-resource settings, poor script-transfer performance) and provide code and hyperparameters, supporting reproducibility. However, there is limited evidence of deeper ablations or sensitivity analyses on the CNN/visual-rendering choices (e.g., font, architecture, training size sweeps) beyond the fusion variants, so some important experimental avenues remain unexamined.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents solid and reasonably comprehensive experiments: multilingual CJK datasets (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) with 12-category Wikipedia classification, direct comparisons to a Lookup baseline, three fusion strategies, and qualitative analyses showing visual-focus and rare-character benefits. It also reports hyperparameters and releases code, and discusses limitations (CNN struggles with low-resource settings and script-transfer drops). However, it lacks deeper ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., on CNN architecture, image rendering choices, and more systematic low-resource/transfer experiments) that would strengthen the claims about when and why the visual model helps.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem—character-level compositionality for CJK languages—by modeling characters as rendered images with a CNN and demonstrates concrete gains: visual embeddings improve handling of rare characters and are complementary to standard lookup embeddings, with fusion methods yielding consistent improvements on large Chinese, Japanese, and Korean Wikipedia datasets. Qualitative analyses showing semantically coherent visual spaces and the availability of code increase the potential for adoption. Limitations (reduced benefit in low-resource regimes and poor transfer between simplified/traditional scripts) temper universality but do not undercut the approach’s potential to change how researchers build character representations and to open new cross-script and multimodal research directions. | B(3): Modeling character-level compositionality with CNN-derived visual embeddings for CJK scripts addresses a concrete practical problem (handling rare/logographic characters) and the paper shows consistent improvements and complementary gains when fused with standard lookup embeddings. The use of large Wikipedia datasets across Chinese, Japanese, and Korean and the availability of code increase reproducibility and potential uptake. However, limitations—reduced benefit in low-resource settings and poor cross-script transfer between simplified/traditional Chinese—suggest the contribution is useful and likely to spur follow-up work but is not transformative for the whole field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—character-level compositionality for CJK languages—by modeling characters as rendered images with a CNN and demonstrates concrete gains: visual embeddings improve handling of rare characters and are complementary to standard lookup embeddings, with fusion methods yielding consistent improvements on large Chinese, Japanese, and Korean Wikipedia datasets. Qualitative analyses showing semantically coherent visual spaces and the availability of code increase the potential for adoption. Limitations (reduced benefit in low-resource regimes and poor transfer between simplified/traditional scripts) temper universality but do not undercut the approach’s potential to change how researchers build character representations and to open new cross-script and multimodal research directions.

reviewer_b.justification: Modeling character-level compositionality with CNN-derived visual embeddings for CJK scripts addresses a concrete practical problem (handling rare/logographic characters) and the paper shows consistent improvements and complementary gains when fused with standard lookup embeddings. The use of large Wikipedia datasets across Chinese, Japanese, and Korean and the availability of code increase reproducibility and potential uptake. However, limitations—reduced benefit in low-resource settings and poor cross-script transfer between simplified/traditional Chinese—suggest the contribution is useful and likely to spur follow-up work but is not transformative for the whole field.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—character-level representation learning—by introducing visual-character embeddings and evaluating them on multilingual CJK text classification, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. Methods (CNN for visual embeddings + RNN encoder), datasets (large Wikipedia-derived multilingual splits), baseline comparisons, evaluation metrics, hyperparameter reporting, and code release match the standards expected at such conferences. The multilingual focus and demonstrated complementary gains with standard embeddings make it relevant to the venue’s audience and research community, despite noted limitations in low-resource and cross-script transfer settings. | B(5): The paper addresses an in-scope NLP/ML topic—learning character-level compositionality via visual character embeddings—and applies standard ML methods (CNN for character images, GRU encoder, softmax classifier) that are of clear interest to the community. Evaluation uses appropriate benchmarks and standards (multilingual Wikipedia-derived classification across Chinese/Japanese/Korean, comparison to character lookup baselines, fusion methods, accuracy metrics) with code and hyperparameters reported. Given the multilingual focus, relevant baselines, and standard evaluation protocol, this work is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—character-level representation learning—by introducing visual-character embeddings and evaluating them on multilingual CJK text classification, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. Methods (CNN for visual embeddings + RNN encoder), datasets (large Wikipedia-derived multilingual splits), baseline comparisons, evaluation metrics, hyperparameter reporting, and code release match the standards expected at such conferences. The multilingual focus and demonstrated complementary gains with standard embeddings make it relevant to the venue’s audience and research community, despite noted limitations in low-resource and cross-script transfer settings.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an in-scope NLP/ML topic—learning character-level compositionality via visual character embeddings—and applies standard ML methods (CNN for character images, GRU encoder, softmax classifier) that are of clear interest to the community. Evaluation uses appropriate benchmarks and standards (multilingual Wikipedia-derived classification across Chinese/Japanese/Korean, comparison to character lookup baselines, fusion methods, accuracy metrics) with code and hyperparameters reported. Given the multilingual focus, relevant baselines, and standard evaluation protocol, this work is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a distinct perspective by deriving character embeddings from visual renderings via a CNN and using them to model character-level compositionality, which differs from standard lookup embeddings. It clearly differentiates its contributions through comparisons to a Lookup baseline and by proposing fusion methods (early, late, fallback) that yield consistent gains on CJK datasets, particularly for rare characters. While the approach applies existing CNN/RNN techniques, framing character representation as a visual-learning problem and demonstrating complementary gains is a non-trivial and informative extension likely to interest experts. | B(3): The paper introduces a clear new perspective by creating character embeddings from visual renderings of characters via a CNN and integrating them into an RNN classifier, which is different from the standard lookup-only approach described. However, the method is largely a straightforward combination of existing components (image CNNs, sequence RNNs, and fusion strategies) applied to the well-studied problem of character-level representations for CJK languages. The authors do clearly differentiate contributions (visual embeddings, complementary fusion with lookup embeddings) and demonstrate gains on Chinese/Japanese/Korean datasets, so the novelty is moderate rather than highly original.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a distinct perspective by deriving character embeddings from visual renderings via a CNN and using them to model character-level compositionality, which differs from standard lookup embeddings. It clearly differentiates its contributions through comparisons to a Lookup baseline and by proposing fusion methods (early, late, fallback) that yield consistent gains on CJK datasets, particularly for rare characters. While the approach applies existing CNN/RNN techniques, framing character representation as a visual-learning problem and demonstrating complementary gains is a non-trivial and informative extension likely to interest experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a clear new perspective by creating character embeddings from visual renderings of characters via a CNN and integrating them into an RNN classifier, which is different from the standard lookup-only approach described. However, the method is largely a straightforward combination of existing components (image CNNs, sequence RNNs, and fusion strategies) applied to the well-studied problem of character-level representations for CJK languages. The authors do clearly differentiate contributions (visual embeddings, complementary fusion with lookup embeddings) and demonstrate gains on Chinese/Japanese/Korean datasets, so the novelty is moderate rather than highly original.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from motivation to method (CNN visual embeddings → RNN classifier) and experiments on CJK Wikipedia datasets; the main contributions are stated early and consistently (visual character embeddings, complementary fusion methods). Notation and methodological description appear consistent and well-defined given the reported model components (CNN, GRU, fusion types) and hyperparameters. Figures/tables and examples seem informative enough to support qualitative analysis and dataset reporting, and overall writing quality is described as good, suggesting only minor presentation issues rather than major clarity problems. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from method (CNN-rendered visual character embeddings) to experiments (multilingual CJK Wikipedia classification) to conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (modeling character-level compositionality via visual features and fusion with lookup embeddings). Notation and methodological details appear adequately specified (CNN+GRU architecture, hyperparameters reported) and figures/qualitative analyses are informative about what the visual model attends to. Writing quality is described as good with clear dataset descriptions and baselines, so only minor presentation issues remain rather than substantive clarity problems.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from motivation to method (CNN visual embeddings → RNN classifier) and experiments on CJK Wikipedia datasets; the main contributions are stated early and consistently (visual character embeddings, complementary fusion methods). Notation and methodological description appear consistent and well-defined given the reported model components (CNN, GRU, fusion types) and hyperparameters. Figures/tables and examples seem informative enough to support qualitative analysis and dataset reporting, and overall writing quality is described as good, suggesting only minor presentation issues rather than major clarity problems.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from method (CNN-rendered visual character embeddings) to experiments (multilingual CJK Wikipedia classification) to conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (modeling character-level compositionality via visual features and fusion with lookup embeddings). Notation and methodological details appear adequately specified (CNN+GRU architecture, hyperparameters reported) and figures/qualitative analyses are informative about what the visual model attends to. Writing quality is described as good with clear dataset descriptions and baselines, so only minor presentation issues remain rather than substantive clarity problems.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The core claims are empirically supported: the Visual CNN character embeddings and fusion methods are evaluated against a Lookup baseline on sizable Wikipedia-derived CJK datasets and show improvements, and qualitative analyses are reported. However, the experimental methodology has gaps (no mention of statistical significance testing and limited baselines—e.g., sub-character/radical or subword methods—that would strengthen claims), and the authors themselves report clear limitations in low-resource and cross-script transfer settings. There are no mathematical derivations to evaluate, and overall the evidence mostly supports the conclusions despite these minor methodological concerns. | B(4): The main claims are generally supported by experiments reported on sizable Wikipedia-derived CJK datasets (accuracy comparisons between the Visual CNN model, Lookup baseline, and fusion methods) and qualitative analyses; hyperparameters and code availability are also provided. However, the experimental methodology appears incomplete from the summary — only a single Lookup baseline is mentioned and no statistical significance testing is reported — which leaves room for alternative explanations for the gains. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the authors acknowledge important limitations (data-hungry CNN, cross-script transfer drops) that temper the conclusions. Overall the technical contribution appears sound but with minor methodological shortcomings that do not overturn the main findings.

reviewer_a.justification: The core claims are empirically supported: the Visual CNN character embeddings and fusion methods are evaluated against a Lookup baseline on sizable Wikipedia-derived CJK datasets and show improvements, and qualitative analyses are reported. However, the experimental methodology has gaps (no mention of statistical significance testing and limited baselines—e.g., sub-character/radical or subword methods—that would strengthen claims), and the authors themselves report clear limitations in low-resource and cross-script transfer settings. There are no mathematical derivations to evaluate, and overall the evidence mostly supports the conclusions despite these minor methodological concerns.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are generally supported by experiments reported on sizable Wikipedia-derived CJK datasets (accuracy comparisons between the Visual CNN model, Lookup baseline, and fusion methods) and qualitative analyses; hyperparameters and code availability are also provided. However, the experimental methodology appears incomplete from the summary — only a single Lookup baseline is mentioned and no statistical significance testing is reported — which leaves room for alternative explanations for the gains. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the authors acknowledge important limitations (data-hungry CNN, cross-script transfer drops) that temper the conclusions. Overall the technical contribution appears sound but with minor methodological shortcomings that do not overturn the main findings.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates the proposed Visual model against a standard character embedding Lookup baseline on the same Wikipedia-derived 12-category Chinese/Japanese/Korean datasets and metrics, and explores fusion variants; it also reports and acknowledges cases where the Visual model underperforms (low-resource training, script transfer). However, the empirical comparisons are limited to the Lookup baseline (plus internal fusion variants), and do not include other relevant alternatives such as radical/sub-character embedding methods or other recent character/byte-level neural baselines, so important competing approaches are missing despite adequate related-work discussion. | B(3): The paper compares its Visual CNN approach primarily against a standard character Lookup baseline on the same datasets, splits, and accuracy metric (fair conditions) and reports fusion improvements and known limitations (e.g., low-resource and script-transfer drops). However, it appears to omit other important character/sub-character baselines (e.g., radical/stroke-based or other recent sub-character methods and byte-level approaches), so comparisons are incomplete despite adequate related-work coverage and fair experimental setup.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates the proposed Visual model against a standard character embedding Lookup baseline on the same Wikipedia-derived 12-category Chinese/Japanese/Korean datasets and metrics, and explores fusion variants; it also reports and acknowledges cases where the Visual model underperforms (low-resource training, script transfer). However, the empirical comparisons are limited to the Lookup baseline (plus internal fusion variants), and do not include other relevant alternatives such as radical/sub-character embedding methods or other recent character/byte-level neural baselines, so important competing approaches are missing despite adequate related-work discussion.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its Visual CNN approach primarily against a standard character Lookup baseline on the same datasets, splits, and accuracy metric (fair conditions) and reports fusion improvements and known limitations (e.g., low-resource and script-transfer drops). However, it appears to omit other important character/sub-character baselines (e.g., radical/stroke-based or other recent sub-character methods and byte-level approaches), so comparisons are incomplete despite adequate related-work coverage and fair experimental setup.

main_claims: We model character-level compositionality by creating embeddings from visual renderings of characters via a CNN and using them in a downstream RNN classifier.
Visual character embeddings improve processing of instances with rare characters in languages such as Chinese, Japanese, and Korean.

The learned visual representations are complementary with standard character embeddings; combining the two via fusion (early, late, fallback) yields consistent improvements over the strongest baseline.
Qualitative analyses show the Visual model focuses on parts of characters that carry categorical content, producing embeddings that are coherent in visual space.
_summary.methodology: Each character is rendered as an image and passed through a Convolutional Neural Network to produce a visual character embedding; sequences of these embeddings are fed into a GRU-based RNN encoder and a softmax classifier. The Visual model is compared to a baseline Lookup (character embedding) model and combined with it via three fusion methods (early, late, fallback).
datasets: Wikipedia-derived 12-category dataset (categories: Geography, Sports, Arts, Military, Economics, Transportation, Health Science, Education, Food Culture, Religion and Belief, Agriculture, Electronics) for Chinese, Japanese, and Korean
Chinese dataset separated into simplified and traditional versions (dataset sizes reported: Chinese 593k articles, Japanese 810k articles, Korean 46.6k articles); train/validation/test split 6:2:2
limitations: No clear performance gain in low-resource training settings because smaller amounts of data adversely impact the CNN’s ability to learn useful visual features.
Performance drops when transferring between scripts (training on traditional Chinese and testing on simplified Chinese yielded ~40% accuracy vs ~55% when trained on simplified).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - character-level representation and text classification (multilingual CJK)
